findings.description: Zip Slip vulnerability in Unpacker.process(): ZipEntry name flows through IdentityNameMapper (no-op) directly to File constructor without canonical path validation, allowing path traversal during extraction.
findings.reasoning: The Unpacker.process() method at line 1149-1152 calls `mapper.map(zipEntry.getName())` where the default mapper is IdentityNameMapper which returns the name unchanged. The mapped name is then used directly in `new File(outputDir, name)` without any canonical path check. A malicious ZIP entry with a name like `../../etc/cron.d/evil` will resolve outside the outputDir. There is no getCanonicalPath(), Path.toRealPath(), or startsWith() containment check anywhere in this method.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: ZipEntry.getName() -> IdentityNameMapper.map() (returns unchanged) -> new File(outputDir, name) -> FileUtils.copy() writes content
evidence.notes: IdentityNameMapper.map() simply returns the input name as-is: `public String map(String name) { return name; }`. No validation exists.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides a malicious ZIP file with traversal paths in entry names; ZipUtil.unpack() writes entries to arbitrary filesystem locations outside the intended output directory.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to supply a ZIP file to the application that calls ZipUtil.unpack()

findings.description: Zip Slip vulnerability in BackslashUnpacker.process(): ZipEntry name split on backslashes is used to construct File objects without canonical path validation, allowing path traversal during extraction.
findings.reasoning: BackslashUnpacker.process() splits the ZipEntry name on backslashes and iterates through the directory components, creating File objects at each step (line 1214) and ultimately the destination file (line 1220). No canonical path check is performed at any point. An entry named `..\\..\\..\\.\\etc\\cron.d\\evil` on Windows, or even directory components containing `..` like `normal\\..\\..\\etc\\evil`, can escape the output directory. The mapper is IdentityNameMapper which returns the name unchanged.

evidence.notes: No canonical path check. An entry like 'a\..\..\..\etc\evil' would traverse out of outputDir.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides a ZIP with backslash-separated path entries containing traversal sequences; BackslashUnpacker splits on backslash and creates nested directories including '..' components, escaping the output directory.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a ZIP file processed by BackslashUnpacker (used for ZIP archives with backslash directory separators)

findings.description: Zip Slip vulnerability in BackslashUnpacker.process(): ZipEntry name (non-backslash case) flows directly to File constructor without canonical path validation.
findings.reasoning: In BackslashUnpacker.process(), when the entry name does NOT contain backslashes (the else branch at line 1222+), the name (from IdentityNameMapper, unchanged from ZipEntry) is used directly in `new File(outputDir, name)` with no canonical path check. An entry name like `../../../etc/evil` will escape outputDir. No validation exists.

evidence.notes: This is the non-backslash branch of BackslashUnpacker - for forward-slash entries, the name goes directly to File constructor.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies ZIP with forward-slash traversal path entry processed by BackslashUnpacker's else-branch, writing to arbitrary filesystem location.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: ZIP file processed via BackslashUnpacker code path

findings.description: Zip Slip vulnerability in Unwraper.process(): ZipEntry name after root-prefix stripping flows to File constructor without canonical path validation, allowing path traversal during unwrap extraction.
findings.reasoning: In Unwraper.process(), the entry name has its root directory stripped via `getUnrootedName()` (a simple `substring()` call), then passed through IdentityNameMapper, and finally used in `new File(outputDir, name)` at line 1260. The root-stripping only removes the first path component - it does NOT prevent traversal via `../` in subsequent components. An entry like `root/../../etc/evil` would strip `root/` to give `../etc/evil`, which then escapes outputDir. No canonical path or startsWith containment check is performed.

evidence.notes: getUnrootedName() is a simple substring, not a traversal sanitizer. Entry 'root/../../etc/evil' -> unrooted '../../etc/evil' -> File(outputDir, '../../etc/evil') escapes outputDir.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides a ZIP processed by ZipUtil.unwrap() with traversal sequences after the root directory; root-stripping leaves traversal sequences that escape the output directory.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a ZIP file to an application calling ZipUtil.unwrap()

findings.description: Zip Slip vulnerability in UnpackingCallback.process(): ZipEntry name used directly (no mapper) to construct File without any canonical path validation, allowing path traversal during extraction.
findings.reasoning: In UnpackingCallback.process(), `entryName = zipEntry.getName()` is used directly (no mapper, no transformation) at line 683 in `new File(destination, entryName)`. There is absolutely no canonical path check, no startsWith containment check, and no validation of traversal sequences. This is the worst case - no sanitization whatsoever. An entry name like `../../etc/passwd` will resolve to a path completely outside the destination directory. Multiple queries (Q2, Q3, Q4, Q5) independently confirmed this has NO_CHECK for containment validation.

evidence.notes: Multiple queries confirm NO_CHECK - no startsWith, getCanonicalPath, or toRealPath anywhere in this method. entryName flows directly from zipEntry.getName() to File constructor.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides a malicious ZIP to Zips.unpack(); UnpackingCallback uses ZipEntry.getName() directly with no sanitization to construct File, writing content to arbitrary filesystem locations.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must supply a ZIP file to an application calling Zips.get(...).unpack()

findings.description: False positive: FileUtils.getTempFileFor() constructs a temp file from an existing File's parent and name - not from ZipEntry name directly.
findings.reasoning: This method receives an existing File object and derives parent and name from it. It does not directly accept ZipEntry names. The `name` variable is `file.getName()` (just the filename component, no path separators), and `parent` is `file.getParentFile()`. This is a utility method for generating unique temp filenames. Even if the input File was tainted, this method only appends an index suffix to an existing filename. Not a Zip Slip vulnerability.

evidence.notes: file.getName() returns only the filename component (no directory separators), so no path traversal is possible here.

findings.description: False positive: FileUtilsV2_2.copyFileToDirectory() uses srcFile.getName() (filename only) to construct destination - not a ZipEntry name and no path traversal possible.
findings.reasoning: This method uses `srcFile.getName()` which returns only the filename component (no directory separators). The input is an existing File object (srcFile), not a ZipEntry name. File.getName() strips all path components and returns only the final name element, making path traversal impossible regardless of how srcFile was constructed. This is a general file utility method, not a ZIP extraction method.

evidence.notes: File.getName() returns only the last component of the path - cannot contain directory traversal sequences.

findings.description: False positive: FileUtilsV2_2.isSymlink() constructs a File using getCanonicalFile() on the parent - this is a canonical path check utility, not vulnerable.
findings.reasoning: This method is specifically designed to detect symlinks by comparing canonical vs absolute paths. It calls `file.getParentFile().getCanonicalFile()` to get the canonical parent directory, then constructs a File with `file.getName()` (filename only, no path separators). This is not a vulnerable File construction - it's using canonical resolution as a security check. The purpose of this method is to detect path manipulation (symlinks), not to write files.

evidence.notes: Uses getCanonicalFile() for symlink detection - this IS the canonical check, not a missing one. file.getName() cannot contain path separators.

findings.description: False positive: ZipUtil.pack() constructs File from directory listing to READ files for compression - not ZIP extraction, no Zip Slip risk.
findings.reasoning: This is in the pack() method which CREATES a ZIP archive by reading from the filesystem - not an extraction operation. The `filename` is obtained from `dir.list()` which lists actual existing files in a trusted directory on the server side, not from ZipEntry names in an untrusted archive. The File object is used to READ content to compress into the ZIP, not to WRITE attacker-controlled content. Zip Slip only applies to extraction (writing files from ZIP), not packing (reading files to ZIP).

evidence.notes: This is a compression/packing operation. filenames come from dir.list() (server filesystem), not from user-supplied ZipEntry names. No attacker-controlled write to filesystem.
